verses.text: Jesus summoned his twelve disciples and gave them authority to cast out unclean spirits and heal every disease and sickness.
These are the names of the twelve apostles: first, Simon (called Peter), and Andrew his brother; James son of Zebedee and John his brother;
Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James son of Alphaeus and Thaddaeus;
Simon the Zealot and Judas Iscariot, the one who betrayed him.
He sent them out with these instructions: ‘Do not go on the road to the Gentiles, and do not enter a city of the Samaritans;
Instead, go to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.
As you are going, proclaim, saying: ‘The kingdom of heaven has come near.’
Heal the sick, cleanse lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received; freely give.
Do not acquire gold, silver, or copper for your money belts;
no traveler’s bag for the road, nor two tunics, nor sandals, nor a staff, for the worker is deserving of his provisions.’
‘Into whatever town or village you enter, inquire who is worthy in it, and stay there until you leave.
And when you enter the house, greet it.
If the house is worthy, let your peace come upon it; but if not, let your peace return to you.
And whoever does not welcome you or listen to your words, shake off the dust from your feet as you go out of that house or town.
Truly I tell you, it will be more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment than for that town.’
‘Behold, I am sending you out like sheep in the midst of wolves. Therefore be wise as serpents and innocent as doves.
But beware of people, because they will hand you over to councils, and they will flog you in their synagogues.
You will be brought before governors and kings because of me, as a testimony to them and to the Gentiles.
But whenever they hand you over, do not be anxious how to speak or what you should say, for what you should say will be given to you at that hour.
For you are not the ones who are speaking, but the Spirit of your Father who is speaking through you.
A family member will betray another to death, and a father his child; children will rise against parents and have them killed.
You will be hated by everyone because of my name, but the one who endures to the end will be saved.
When they persecute you in this town, flee to another. Truly I tell you, you will never finish going through the towns of Israel until the Son of Man comes.’
‘A disciple is not greater than their teacher, nor a slave greater than their master.
It is sufficient for a disciple to be like their teacher, and the slave like their master. If they have called the master of the house Beelzebul, how much more the members of his household!
Therefore do not fear them, because nothing is hidden that will not be revealed, and nothing secret that will not become known.
What I tell you in the dark, speak in the light, and what you hear in your ear, proclaim from the rooftops.
Do not fear those who kill the body but are not able to kill the soul. Rather, fear Him who can destroy both soul and body in Gehenna.
Are not two sparrows sold for an assarion? Yet not one of them falls to the ground apart from your Father.
And even the hairs of your head are all numbered.
Therefore, do not fear. You are worth more than many sparrows.’
‘Everyone who acknowledges me before others, I will also acknowledge before my Father who is in heaven.
But whoever denies me before others, I will also deny before my Father who is in heaven.
Do not think I came to bring peace on the earth; I did not come to bring peace, but a sword.
For I have come to divide a man against his father, and a daughter against her mother, and a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.
A person’s enemies will be members of their household.
Anyone who loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me; and anyone who loves son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me.
Whoever does not take up their cross and follow me is not worthy of me.
Whoever finds their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life for my sake will find it.
‘Whoever receives you receives me, and whoever receives me receives Him who sent me.
Whoever receives a prophet because they are a prophet will receive a prophet’s reward, and whoever receives a righteous person because they are righteous will receive a righteous person’s reward.
And whoever gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because they are my disciple, truly I tell you, they will never lose their reward.’

footnotes.content: “Provisions” refers to the basic needs of those doing God's work—such as food, shelter, and clothing. Jesus affirms that those who serve the kingdom should be supported by those they minister to.

The gesture of shaking dust from one's feet was a symbolic act of renouncing responsibility and fellowship with that place. In Jewish custom, it was sometimes performed when leaving Gentile territory, expressing a break in spiritual solidarity. Here, it underscores the severity of rejecting the kingdom message. It marks that town as covenantally excluded.

This comparative phrase expresses a severe divine judgment using the infamous fate of Sodom and Gomorrah as a benchmark. In context, it heightens the spiritual accountability of those who reject the message brought by Jesus' messengers. It also implies that judgment is graduated, not uniform. This is a key point in Jesus' theology of divine justice.

This verse contains one of the most debated sayings of Jesus. The phrase “before the Son of Man comes” can be understood in several ways: as a reference to (1) Jesus' resurrection and post-resurrection appearances, (2) His coming in judgment against Jerusalem in 70 AD, or (3) His final return at the end of the age. The immediate context favors a near-term fulfillment—Jesus comforting His disciples during a time of Jewish mission and persecution. The phrase may reflect a recurring pattern in prophetic speech where one event (e.g., judgment on Israel) foreshadows a greater one (the final coming). Rather than specifying chronology, Jesus assures His disciples that their mission will not be in vain and that He will come in power before their work is exhausted.

When Jesus says, “If they have called the master of the house Beelzebul,” he refers to accusations that he worked by demonic power (see Matthew 12:24). The term “Beelzebul” likely mocks Baal as “lord of dung.” His point is that disciples should not expect better treatment than their master. If Jesus himself was slandered, his followers will face even worse. The phrase “members of his household” emphasizes spiritual kinship—and shared suffering.

Assarion (assarion) was a minor Roman bronze coin, roughly equivalent to 1/16 of a denarius, used for small transactions such as the sale of sparrows. The phrase “not one falls to the ground apart from your Father” (Greek: aneu tou patros hymōn) is open-ended and lacks an explicit verb like “wills” or “knows.” This leaves room for interpretive nuance: it could imply divine will, knowledge, presence, or permission. The ambiguity underscores the theological emphasis on God's intimate involvement in even the smallest events, without specifying the mode of that involvement.

The statement “Even the hairs of your head are all numbered” uses hyperbole to express God's intimate care. In Jewish idiom, counting one's hairs meant absolute attentiveness—nothing escapes his knowledge. Jesus assures that even what seems trivial is known and valued by the Father.

Jesus' statement is not a call to violence but a warning about the unavoidable division caused by the truth of the Gospel. The “sword” represents the piercing nature of God's Word and the Truth it reveals—dividing light from darkness, loyalty from rebellion, and even kin from kin. The Greek word machaira refers to a small sword or dagger, and in biblical usage, it often symbolizes judgment or spiritual separation (cf. Hebrews 4:12). Jesus is declaring that allegiance to Him will expose and fracture even the closest earthly relationships, just as prophesied in Micah 7:6. This sword is the dividing force of Truth—not violence, but conviction.

The phrase “I have come to divide a man against his father…” echoes Micah 7:6, where the breakdown of family trust is described as a symptom of societal collapse. Jesus is not promoting conflict but revealing that allegiance to him often provokes division, even within the most sacred earthly bonds. Following him demands a higher loyalty than cultural or familial expectations. His coming exposes hearts and forces a choice—creating division not by aggression, but by truth.

The phrase “Whoever finds their life will lose it…” expresses the paradox at the heart of discipleship. To “find your life” means to cling to ego—your self-centered identity, your control, your ambitions. But in grasping for self, you forfeit what matters most: life rooted in God. Conversely, “losing your life for my sake” is not merely about martyrdom—it's the daily surrender of ego, the laying down of personal sovereignty to receive divine purpose. In dying to the false self, you awaken to the true life God offers. Jesus invites us to trade the fragile illusion of control for the indestructible freedom of trust.

The phrase “will receive a prophet's reward” refers to the shared blessing that comes from honoring a prophet's mission. In Jewish tradition, a prophet was God's mouthpiece, and to receive one—meaning to support, host, or heed their message—was to participate in their sacred work. Jesus affirms that even those who aren't prophets themselves but honor one because they recognize their role will share in their spiritual reward. The same applies to “a righteous person”—this speaks to how God notices not only those on the front lines, but also those who stand beside them in faith and solidarity.

The phrase “a cup of cold water to one of these little ones” highlights how even the smallest act of kindness toward a disciple—motivated by their identity in Christ—is seen and rewarded by God. “Little ones” refers not to children, but to seemingly insignificant followers. Jesus affirms that no act of love done for his sake is ever overlooked.
